title: Harvard Glee Club

infobox.value: (**Glee Club** or **HGC**) is a 60-voice, tenor-bass [choral](/wiki/Choir "Choir") ensemble at [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University"). Founded in [1858](/wiki/1858_in_music "1858 in music") in the tradition of English and American [glee clubs](/wiki/Glee_club "Glee club"), it is the oldest collegiate chorus in the United States.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The Glee Club is part of the [Harvard Choruses](/wiki/Harvard_Choruses "Harvard Choruses") of Harvard University, which also include the treble voice [Radcliffe Choral Society](/wiki/Radcliffe_Choral_Society "Radcliffe Choral Society") and the mixed-voice [Harvard-Radcliffe Collegium Musicum](/wiki/Harvard-Radcliffe_Collegium_Musicum "Harvard-Radcliffe Collegium Musicum"). All three groups are led by Harvard's current director of choral activities Andrew Clark.

Founded in [1858](/wiki/1858_in_music "1858 in music") in the tradition of English and American [glee clubs](/wiki/Glee_club "Glee club"), it is the oldest collegiate chorus in the United States.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The Glee Club is part of the [Harvard Choruses](/wiki/Harvard_Choruses "Harvard Choruses") of Harvard University, which also include the treble voice [Radcliffe Choral Society](/wiki/Radcliffe_Choral_Society "Radcliffe Choral Society") and the mixed-voice [Harvard-Radcliffe Collegium Musicum](/wiki/Harvard-Radcliffe_Collegium_Musicum "Harvard-Radcliffe Collegium Musicum"). All three groups are led by Harvard's current director of choral activities Andrew Clark.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Choral ensemble

| Harvard Glee Club | |
| --- | --- |
| [Choir](/wiki/Choir "Choir") | |
| [Harvard Glee Club seal](/wiki/File:Glee_Club_Harvard.svg) | |
| Origin | [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts"), United States |
| Founded | 1858; 168 years ago (1858) |
| President | Jake Truncale '27 |
| Music director | Andrew Clark[[1]](#cite_note-1) |
| Manager | Samson Axelrod '28 |
| Rehearsal space | [Holden Chapel](/wiki/Holden_Chapel "Holden Chapel") |
| Concert hall | [Sanders Theatre](/wiki/Sanders_Theatre "Sanders Theatre") |
| Affiliation | [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") |

| Website | [harvardgleeclub.org](https://harvardgleeclub.org/) |

The **Harvard Glee Club** (**Glee Club** or **HGC**) is a 60-voice, tenor-bass [choral](/wiki/Choir "Choir") ensemble at [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University"). Founded in [1858](/wiki/1858_in_music "1858 in music") in the tradition of English and American [glee clubs](/wiki/Glee_club "Glee club"), it is the oldest collegiate chorus in the United States.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The Glee Club is part of the [Harvard Choruses](/wiki/Harvard_Choruses "Harvard Choruses") of Harvard University, which also include the treble voice [Radcliffe Choral Society](/wiki/Radcliffe_Choral_Society "Radcliffe Choral Society") and the mixed-voice [Harvard-Radcliffe Collegium Musicum](/wiki/Harvard-Radcliffe_Collegium_Musicum "Harvard-Radcliffe Collegium Musicum"). All three groups are led by Harvard's current director of choral activities Andrew Clark.

The Glee Club has long been a fixture of the [Boston](/wiki/Boston,_Massachusetts "Boston, Massachusetts") music scene, performing frequently with the [Boston Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Boston_Symphony_Orchestra "Boston Symphony Orchestra") and other ensembles, but this local prominence has lessened in recent years.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, thanks to over 80 annual tours to different regions of the United States and appearances at the Kennedy Center Honors and in [Leonard Bernstein](/wiki/Leonard_Bernstein "Leonard Bernstein")'s popular series *The Unanswered Question*, the Glee Club has garnered national recognition; tours around the world have brought the group further attention. A number of notable people were members of the Glee Club during their time at Harvard, and numerous major composers of the 20th and 21st centuries have dedicated works to the group.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harvard_Glee_Club&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Founding and development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harvard_Glee_Club&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Founding and development")]

The Glee Club was founded in 1858 by a group of students to sing [glees](/wiki/Glee_(music) "Glee (music)") and part-songs. The group remained small until the end of the nineteenth century, when growth in its size and on-campus profile made higher musical aspirations possible. In 1919, it invited Archibald T. "Doc" Davison, the choirmaster of Harvard's [Memorial Church](/wiki/Memorial_Church_of_Harvard_University "Memorial Church of Harvard University"), to become Glee Club conductor. In [1921](/wiki/1921_in_music "1921 in music"), the Glee Club embarked on its first European tour, which, though not the first such tour by a college group, was the most extensive to that point. The group was officially invited by the government of [France](/wiki/France "France"), and the tour was covered by the press in the US and Europe.[[3]](#cite_note-3) This tour also resulted in a spate of new work written expressly for the Glee Club by such composers as [Darius Milhaud](/wiki/Darius_Milhaud "Darius Milhaud"), [Francis Poulenc](/wiki/Francis_Poulenc "Francis Poulenc"), and [Gustav Holst](/wiki/Gustav_Holst "Gustav Holst").[[4]](#cite_note-Kreger-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Hoover_with_Harvard_Glee_Club%2C_4-8-29_LCCN2016843635.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Hoover_with_Harvard_Glee_Club%2C_4-8-29_LCCN2016843635.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hoover_with_Harvard_Glee_Club,_4-8-29_LCCN2016843635.tif)

President [Herbert Hoover](/wiki/Herbert_Hoover "Herbert Hoover") with Harvard Glee Club on April 8, 1929

Under Davison, the Glee Club and the [Radcliffe Choral Society](/wiki/Radcliffe_Choral_Society "Radcliffe Choral Society") became the choruses of choice for the [Boston Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Boston_Symphony_Orchestra "Boston Symphony Orchestra") and frequently recorded with them. Their recording of *[La Damnation de Faust](/wiki/La_Damnation_de_Faust "La Damnation de Faust")* won a Grand Prix du Disc, and a recording of the [Mozart Requiem](/wiki/Mozart_Requiem "Mozart Requiem") in memory of former [U.S. President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") and Harvard graduate [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") received a nomination for a [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy "Grammy").[[4]](#cite_note-Kreger-4) The relationship with the BSO continued until the creation of the [Tanglewood Festival Chorus](/wiki/Tanglewood_Festival_Chorus "Tanglewood Festival Chorus"); both the Club and Society continue to sing with the BSO on occasion.

Since the retirement of Doc Davison, the Glee Club has had only five conductors: G. Wallace "Woody" Woodworth, who led the group from 1933 to 1958; noted Beethoven scholar [Elliot Forbes](/wiki/Elliot_Forbes "Elliot Forbes"), from 1958 to 1970, who led the group on an extensive tour around the world in [1961](/wiki/1961_in_music "1961 in music");[[5]](#cite_note-5) F. John Adams, 1970–1978; [Jameson N. Marvin](/wiki/Jameson_Marvin "Jameson Marvin"), 1978–2010; and Andrew G. Clark through the present.

Under the leadership of Jameson Marvin as conductor of the Glee Club, the group continued to tour extensively, and was invited to a number of conventions of the [American Choral Directors Association](/wiki/American_Choral_Directors_Association "American Choral Directors Association"), invitations that were extended only through a [blind audition](/wiki/Blind_audition "Blind audition") process. The Glee Club appeared at regional conventions in [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh") in 2002 and Boston in 2004 and at a national convention in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") in 2005. Concerts led by Marvin were favorably received across the country and around the world.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Notable alumni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harvard_Glee_Club&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Notable alumni")]

Many Glee Club members and assistant conductors have gone on to become leaders of American music, including composers, choral directors, and orchestra managers across the country. Alumni of the Glee Club notable for careers in music include:

* [Virgil Thomson](/wiki/Virgil_Thomson "Virgil Thomson"), who was assistant conductor on the 1921 European tour[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* [Elliott Carter](/wiki/Elliott_Carter "Elliott Carter"), who remarked, "I owe my knowledge of music to the Harvard Glee Club"[[8]](#cite_note-8)
* [William Christie](/wiki/William_Christie_(musician) "William Christie (musician)"), also briefly the group's accompanist[[9]](#cite_note-Forbes-9)
* [Leonard Bernstein](/wiki/Leonard_Bernstein "Leonard Bernstein")[[4]](#cite_note-Kreger-4)
* [Irving Fine](/wiki/Irving_Fine "Irving Fine")[[10]](#cite_note-10)
* [John Gurney](/wiki/John_Gurney_(bass-baritone) "John Gurney (bass-baritone)"), bass-baritone with the [Metropolitan Opera](/wiki/Metropolitan_Opera "Metropolitan Opera")[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* [John Harbison](/wiki/John_Harbison "John Harbison")[[4]](#cite_note-Kreger-4)
* [Hugh Wolff](/wiki/Hugh_Wolff "Hugh Wolff")[[4]](#cite_note-Kreger-4)
* [William Martin](/wiki/William_Martin_(tenor) "William Martin (tenor)"), operatic tenor
* [Scott Tucker](/wiki/Scott_Tucker_(conductor) "Scott Tucker (conductor)"), current director of the Washington Choral Arts Society.[[4]](#cite_note-Kreger-4)
* [Isaiah Jackson](/wiki/Isaiah_Jackson_(conductor) "Isaiah Jackson (conductor)"), director of [Pro Arte Chamber Orchestra](/wiki/Pro_Arte_Chamber_Orchestra "Pro Arte Chamber Orchestra")[[9]](#cite_note-Forbes-9)
* [Noel Jan Tyl](/wiki/Noel_Jan_Tyl "Noel Jan Tyl"), opera singer and former business manager of [Houston Grand Opera](/wiki/Houston_Grand_Opera "Houston Grand Opera")

In addition, a number of Harvard Glee Club alumni have gone on to distinguished careers in other areas. They include:

* [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt"), twenty-sixth [President of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States")[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* [Franklin Delano Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_Delano_Roosevelt "Franklin Delano Roosevelt"), thirty-second [President of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States")[[13]](#cite_note-13)
* [Harry Blackmun](/wiki/Harry_Blackmun "Harry Blackmun"), former Supreme Court Justice and author of the majority opinion in *[Roe v. Wade](/wiki/Roe_v._Wade "Roe v. Wade")* [[14]](#cite_note-14)
* [Jack Reed](/wiki/John_Silas_Reed "John Silas Reed"), author of *[Ten Days that Shook the World](/wiki/Ten_Days_that_Shook_the_World "Ten Days that Shook the World")*, who was also President of the Harvard Glee Club
* [Jeff Bingaman Jr.](/wiki/Jeff_Bingaman "Jeff Bingaman"), former U.S. Senator from New Mexico
* [G. C. Waldrep](/wiki/G._C._Waldrep "G. C. Waldrep"), American poet
* [Noam Elkies](/wiki/Noam_Elkies "Noam Elkies"), mathematician
* [Patrick Harlan](/wiki/Patrick_Harlan "Patrick Harlan"), television personality in Japan

## Modern era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harvard_Glee_Club&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Modern era")]

The Harvard Glee Club is faculty-directed but entirely student-managed. Each tour and major project, such as a large concert or production and release of a recording, has its own student manager. As such, the students themselves are in charge of selecting concert venues, managing a six-figure yearly budget, and taking care of virtually every facet of the group.

The Glee Club rehearses primarily in [Holden Chapel](/wiki/Holden_Chapel "Holden Chapel") in [Harvard Yard](/wiki/Harvard_Yard "Harvard Yard"). Built in 1744, Holden is one of the oldest college buildings in America.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
The group performs most of its "home" concerts in Harvard's [Sanders Theatre](/wiki/Sanders_Theatre "Sanders Theatre"), which is renowned for its excellent acoustics.
Each year, major concerts include the Harvard–[Princeton](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University") and Harvard–[Yale](/wiki/Yale "Yale") Football Concerts, joint concerts that have taken place the night before these football games for more than a century; frequent concerts also include ones with the [Radcliffe Choral Society](/wiki/Radcliffe_Choral_Society "Radcliffe Choral Society") at Christmas and with all of the Harvard Choruses during Harvard's [Arts First](/wiki/Arts_First "Arts First") celebration in May. The Glee Club tours annually at no cost to its students over school breaks; recent tours have taken the group to [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") (2025), the [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic") (2023), [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida") (2022), and [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") (2019). The Glee Club also takes longer, several-week tours roughly every 4 years. Recent[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] major tours have included trips to [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") (1993), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") (1998), [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") (2002), Central Europe (2005), and Western Europe (2023–4).

The 2007–2008 season marked the 150th anniversary of the Glee Club's founding. Highlights included a week-long tour of the Eastern Seaboard and a three-day festival in Cambridge from April 11–13, 2008. Nearly four hundred alumni of the Glee Club attended the April festivities, which included the world premiere of [Dominick Argento](/wiki/Dominick_Argento "Dominick Argento")'s "Apollo in Cambridge: A Harvard Triptych," a performance of Igor Stravinsky's [Symphony of Psalms](/wiki/Symphony_of_Psalms "Symphony of Psalms") with the combined Holden Choruses and orchestra, seminars on a variety of musical, academic, and historical topics, and a well-attended Sesquicentennial Banquet.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
The anniversary celebration continued into the summer of 2008 with a cross-country concert tour culminating in appearances at [Walt Disney Concert Hall](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Concert_Hall "Walt Disney Concert Hall") and the [Ravinia Festival](/wiki/Ravinia_Festival "Ravinia Festival").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Musical tradition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harvard_Glee_Club&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Musical tradition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Harvard_college_-_annenberg_hall.jpg/250px-Harvard_college_-_annenberg_hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harvard_college_-_annenberg_hall.jpg)

Sanders Theatre at Harvard University, location of many Glee Club concerts

The Glee Club performs a wide range of repertoire. Music of the Renaissance is an integral part of that repertoire, as is folk music, especially of America and Eastern Europe. In recent years,[*[as of?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] the Glee Club has performed numerous major works for male chorus, including [Schubert](/wiki/Franz_Schubert "Franz Schubert")'s *[Gesang der Geister über den Wassern](/wiki/Gesang_der_Geister_%C3%BCber_den_Wassern "Gesang der Geister über den Wassern")*, [Brahms](/wiki/Johannes_Brahms "Johannes Brahms")'s *[Alt-Rhapsodie](/wiki/Alt-Rhapsodie "Alt-Rhapsodie")*, [Schoenberg](/wiki/Arnold_Schoenberg "Arnold Schoenberg")'s *[A Survivor from Warsaw](/wiki/A_Survivor_from_Warsaw "A Survivor from Warsaw")*, and [Argento](/wiki/Dominick_Argento "Dominick Argento")'s *Revelation of St. John the Divine*.

Symphony collaborations over the years have included multiple performances with the [Boston Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Boston_Symphony_Orchestra "Boston Symphony Orchestra") (BSO) under all of its conductors since 1917, as well as with the [New York](/wiki/New_York_Philharmonic "New York Philharmonic") and [Los Angeles Philharmonics](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Philharmonic "Los Angeles Philharmonic"), the Netherlands Chamber Orchestra, the Orchestra of St. Luke's, and the Italian Radio Orchestra. Some BSO highlights include the American premiere of [Stravinsky](/wiki/Igor_Stravinsky "Igor Stravinsky")’s *Oedipus Rex*, later recorded with the BSO under Bernstein, two [Berlioz](/wiki/Hector_Berlioz "Hector Berlioz") recordings (*Romeo et Juliet* and *[La Damnation de Faust](/wiki/La_Damnation_de_Faust "La Damnation de Faust")*), and [Mozart](/wiki/Wolfgang_Amadeus_Mozart "Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart")’s *Requiem*. In 1973, the Glee Club performed Bernstein's [Chichester Psalms](/wiki/Chichester_Psalms "Chichester Psalms") with the composer conducting at the [Vatican](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"). The Glee Club now frequently performs with Boston's Orchestra of Emmanuel Music, including another performance of [Stravinsky](/wiki/Igor_Stravinsky "Igor Stravinsky")’s *Oedipus Rex* on February 23, 2018.[[4]](#cite_note-Kreger-4)

The Glee Club frequently performs traditional Harvard football songs, such as "Yo-Ho," "[Ten Thousand Men of Harvard](/wiki/Ten_Thousand_Men_of_Harvard "Ten Thousand Men of Harvard")," "[Harvardiana](/wiki/Harvardiana_(song) "Harvardiana (song)")," "The Gridiron King," "Soldiers' Field," and "Up the Street."

Singers are divided into four parts: tenor 1, tenor 2, baritone, and bass, though various arrangements often require a few singers to switch parts for balance or for the entire group to re-divide into two, three, or five-part harmony.

### Glee Club Lite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harvard_Glee_Club&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Glee Club Lite")]

The Glee Club also has a subset called "Harvard Glee Club Lite" (or simply "Lite"). This group, which features 12–16 singers and performs pop, jazz, and folk a cappella arrangements, was formed in 1985 to give Glee Club members a chance to sing a wider range of music; Harvard has several a cappella groups on campus, and Lite allows students to experience both types of ensemble—a small, student-directed pop-driven group and a larger, faculty-led choral ensemble.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Generally, much or all of Lite's repertoire is arranged by members. The signature song of the group is "Good Ol' A Cappella" by [the Nylons](/wiki/The_Nylons "The Nylons").

### Commissions and dedications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Harvard_Glee_Club&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Commissions and dedications")]

Another cornerstone of the Glee Club's repertoire is contemporary music; the group has a long history of commissioning or simply receiving work from prominent composers or arrangers, some of whom are listed below with the title of the work when available; many of these works note the dedication to the Glee Club on their title page:

* [Darius Milhaud](/wiki/Darius_Milhaud "Darius Milhaud") (*Psaume 121*)
* [Francis Poulenc](/wiki/Francis_Poulenc "Francis Poulenc") (*Chanson à boire*)
* [Gustav Holst](/wiki/Gustav_Holst "Gustav Holst") ("A Love Song", "How Mighty are the Sabbaths" and "Before Sleep" from *Six Choruses*, 1931–2)
* [Walter Piston](/wiki/Walter_Piston "Walter Piston") (*Carnival Song*)
* [Irving Fine](/wiki/Irving_Fine "Irving Fine") (*Vultur Gryphus* and others)
* [Samuel Adler](/wiki/Samuel_Adler_(composer) "Samuel Adler (composer)") (*Two Songs of Peace*)
* [Paul Moravec](/wiki/Paul_Moravec "Paul Moravec") (*Credo*)
* [Leonard Bernstein](/wiki/Leonard_Bernstein "Leonard Bernstein") (*Dedication*)
* [Elliott Carter](/wiki/Elliott_Carter "Elliott Carter") (*Tarantella*, *The Defense of Corinth*, *Emblems*)
* [John Harbison](/wiki/John_Harbison "John Harbison") (*Nunc Dimittis*)
* [Virgil Thomson](/wiki/Virgil_Thomson "Virgil Thomson") (*Cantantes Eamus*)
* [Randall Thompson](/wiki/Randall_Thompson "Randall Thompson") (*Quis multa gracilis*, *The Peaceable Kingdom* and others)
* [Toru Takemitsu](/wiki/Toru_Takemitsu "Toru Takemitsu") (*Grass*)
* [Morten Lauridsen](/wiki/Morten_Lauridsen "Morten Lauridsen") (*Ave Dulcissima Maria*)
* [Sir John Tavener](/wiki/Sir_John_Tavener "Sir John Tavener") (*Awed by the Beauty*)
* [Stephen Paulus](/wiki/Stephen_Paulus "Stephen Paulus") (*Shall I Compare Thee*)
* [Dominick Argento](/wiki/Dominick_Argento "Dominick Argento") (*Apollo in Cambridge*)
* Carol Barnett (*One Equal Music*)
* [Frank Ferko](/wiki/Frank_Ferko "Frank Ferko") (*O Coruscans Lux Stellarum*)
* [Charles Fussell](/wiki/Charles_Fussell "Charles Fussell") (*A Walt Whitman Sampler*)
* [Tarik O'Regan](/wiki/Tarik_O%27Regan "Tarik O'Regan") (*Se Lamentar Augelli*)
* [P.D.Q. Bach](/wiki/P.D.Q._Bach "P.D.Q. Bach") (*A Veritable Paean of Praise*)
* Brandon Waddles (Three Spirituals: "Lord, I Just Can't Keep from Crying," "I'm a Poor Li'l Orphan in Dis World," "Plenty Good Room")

In addition, the Glee Club's conductors have a long tradition of dedicating folk song arrangements and editions of Renaissance vocal pieces to the group; Jameson Marvin's arrangements are published primarily by Oxford University Publishing and Earthsongs.[[19]](#cite_note-19)
